label: fiction
title: The Shrinkage Score
setting: a suburban supermarket, a loss-prevention dashboard, and one employee's risk score, the present day
elements: Money or stock was stolen from the store
hook: The supermarket's risk model scored Corinne Abello eighty-seven out of a hundred — the highest in the region, a number that read like a verdict. Then the defence asked one question nobody had: what did the shrinkage do during her three weeks of leave? It rose eleven per cent.

accused.human: Corinne Abello, 44. Eight years on the service desk, where she supervises every refund in the store and trained half the staff who process the rest. She found out she had a 'score' at all from a detective.
accused.if_guilty: a theft conviction, eight years of trusted work erased, and unemployability across the whole trade

prosecution.speaker: pc
prosecution.text: The chain's loss-prevention model scores every employee for risk, and Corinne Abello scored the highest in the region: eighty-seven. On her shifts, stock shrinkage ran at more than twice the store's baseline, week after week for a quarter. When the manager pulled the footage, he watched her hover the self-checkouts, open her drawer without sales, void transactions all shift. The number found her. The camera confirmed the shape of it. This is what a modern till thief looks like.

defence.speaker: dc
defence.text: A number is not a witness, and this one measured the wrong thing. Corinne Abello scored high because she works the busiest shifts at the service desk — where every refund in the store crosses her counter, where voids and no-sales are the entire job. Three months of surveillance never caught her taking a single item or dollar. The vendor's own manual calls these scores 'investigative leads, not evidence'. The prosecution mistook a woman's roster for her character. The real thief, you'll find, was arithmetic.

prosecution.speaker: pc
prosecution.text: I accept the audit found faults — a light scale, a double-counting dock. But she voided at three times the average, opened a no-sale drawer twice a shift, has a car loan in arrears and a tight budget, and could not account for her own transactions. The defence says the machine blamed her roster. Perhaps. Or perhaps someone who understood the service desk better than anyone knew exactly where a busy store stops watching. The pattern was real, and it was hers.

defence.speaker: dc
defence.text: The judge will tell you a risk score cannot take the oath, and correlation is where an investigation begins, not where a verdict rests. Remove her — three weeks interstate, boarding passes in evidence — and the theft rose. The audit found a self-checkout scale under-weighing produce since a firmware update, the fault she reported twice, and a dock inventing stock that never existed. She reported the cause months before the model named her the cause. There is no observed theft. There is a spreadsheet. You must acquit.

epilogue: The jury acquitted; the eleven-per-cent line — theft rising while she sat on a beach interstate — was, one juror said, 'the whole trial in a single number.' Corinne was reinstated with back pay but transferred stores; the old one felt, she said, 'like being watched by a ghost that already decided.' The chain suspended the scoring model pending review and fixed scale four. Her two ignored fault tickets were entered into evidence. She now sits on the union committee that negotiates how such systems may — and may not — be used.

cast.name: Corinne Abello
cast.role_label: the accused — service desk supervisor, eight years

beats.speaker: pc
beats.text: Exhibit one: the loss-prevention dashboard. The supermarket chain's risk model scores every single employee, and the accused scored eighty-seven out of a hundred — the highest score in the entire region. The printout is stark: stock shrinkage on her shifts ran at more than twice the store's baseline, week after week, for an entire quarter.
beats.reveal_note: The model scored correlation and everyone downstream read causation. She worked the busiest shifts at the registers where shrinkage happens — so, therefore, did the shrinkage. Eighty-seven measured her roster, not her honesty.

beats.speaker: w2
beats.text: Once head office flagged her, I pulled the camera footage and watched for two nights. She hovers around the self-checkouts constantly. She handles voids and refunds all shift, opens her drawer without sales, moves between registers. Watching it with the score in front of me, honestly, it looked like method.
beats.reveal_note: He watched a service-desk supervisor doing a service-desk job — hovering, voiding, assisting — with a number that said thief propped beside the screen. The score wrote the story; the footage just illustrated it.

beats.speaker: w1
beats.text: The loss-prevention manager agreed the vendor's own manual describes the scores as 'investigative leads, not evidence of wrongdoing'. He agreed nobody at the chain had ever audited what data feeds the model. And he agreed that in three months of watching, no one ever observed the accused take a single item or dollar.
beats.reveal_note: A quarter of surveillance produced no theft, no pocketed note, no bag check failure — only a number, restated with increasing confidence.

beats.speaker: pc
beats.text: Exhibit three: her register activity. Voided transactions at three times the store average, and roughly two no-sale drawer openings every shift. The prosecution says voids are how a till thief skims — ring it up, void it quietly, keep the cash — and that her numbers fit that method precisely.
beats.reveal_note: Service-desk staff process the whole store's refunds — the void count was her job description rendered as statistics. The no-sales were locker change and trolley coins, required by the same head office now prosecuting them.

beats.speaker: dc
beats.text: Exhibit four: the comparison nobody ran until the defence demanded it. During the accused's three weeks of annual leave — interstate, with boarding passes in evidence — shrinkage on her usual shifts did not fall. It rose, by eleven per cent. The pattern the model attributed to her carried on comfortably without her.
beats.reveal_note: The experiment the dashboard never thought to run: remove the suspect, watch the number. The number went up. Whatever was leaking, it was not her.

beats.speaker: w3
beats.text: The audit took nine days to find what three months of surveillance missed. Self-checkout scale four had under-weighed loose produce by around nine per cent since a firmware update — on busy shifts it processes thousands of items. And the receiving dock had been double-counting one supplier's deliveries, creating phantom stock that could never be sold. The theft was arithmetic.
beats.reveal_note: A light scale and a double-counted dock: two boring faults that manufactured a crime wave, and a model happy to pin it on whoever worked the most Saturdays.

beats.speaker: w4
beats.text: The service desk is voids — that is the entire job. Every refund in that store crosses my counter, and I trained half the staff who process the rest. I reported scale four weighing light twice myself; I have the ticket numbers in my diary. Eight years I have worked there. I found out my own score from a detective.
beats.reveal_note: She reported the actual cause of the shrinkage twice, months before the model blamed her for it. Both tickets were closed without anyone testing the scale.

beats.speaker: w4
beats.text: 'You have a car loan in arrears? A tight budget?' 'Like most of my colleagues, yes.' 'Can you account for every void you processed last quarter?' 'Every single one, across three months?' 'Answer the question.' 'No. Can you account for every email you sent? Can anyone—' 'Just answer the questions, please,' the prosecutor said, and moved on quickly.
beats.reveal_note: A tight budget shared with half the workforce, and an impossible memory test no honest cashier could pass — the cross-examination proved she was ordinary, not guilty.

beats.speaker: w2
beats.text: The store manager agreed he only ever reviewed footage the system flagged, never a random sample. He agreed her two scale-fault tickets were closed 'no fault found' without the scale being tested. And he agreed that after her suspension, he assigned her void workload to three separate people — because it was too much for one.
beats.reveal_note: It took three employees to replace the workload that the dashboard had scored as one woman's crime spree.

beats.speaker: judge
beats.text: You must not research this case, this employer, or these scoring systems anywhere online. A risk score is not a witness: it cannot be cross-examined, and its confidence is not evidence of anything. Correlation is where an investigation begins, not where a verdict rests. The prosecution must make you sure that she stole — an observed act, not an inferred pattern.
beats.reveal_note: The direction the era needed: a score cannot take the oath. Somewhere between the scale and the dock, the actual facts had been waiting the whole time.

twist: Eighty-seven out of a hundred sounded like science, and it measured her roster: the busiest shifts, the void-heavy service desk, the self-checkouts she was paid to supervise. When she took three weeks' leave, the 'theft' rose eleven per cent without her. The real culprits were a scale under-weighing produce since a firmware update — the fault she herself reported twice — and a receiving dock double-counting deliveries. The model found the person most correlated with the store being busy. The store was always busy when things went missing. So was she.

jurors.label: Juror 2
jurors.persona: Eighty-seven out of a hundred sounds like a grade; he trusts grades.

agree: Highest score in the region. Regions have thousands of employees. Numbers like that mean something.
Twice the baseline shrinkage, every week she worked. The pattern is the case.
pushback: Companies do not buy million-dollar systems that accuse people at random.
You all keep explaining away the score. Someone explain the missing money instead.
concede: The missing money went up while she was on a beach interstate. The score never noticed.
final: I trusted the grade. Nobody told me the exam was rigged by a broken scale.

jurors.label: Juror 3
jurors.persona: Data analyst; has watched dashboards launder coincidence into confidence for a living.

agree: Remove the variable, rerun the experiment: shrinkage rose without her. That is the cleanest test in the whole trial, and the defence had to beg for it.
The vendor's manual says leads, not evidence. The vendor knows its own model.
pushback: Nobody audited the inputs. A model fed a busy roster will flag the busiest person forever.
Show me one observed theft. A quarter of cameras. One.
concede: Her void rate is genuinely extreme — until you learn what desk she runs.
final: I build these dashboards. You should hear how we talk about them internally.

jurors.label: Juror 4
jurors.persona: The principled holdout; 'no observed act' is where he plants the flag.

agree: Precisely — an observed act, not an inferred pattern. The direction is the verdict's skeleton.
pushback: Three months of surveillance and not one theft on camera. Sit with that before quoting the score again.
A number that cannot be cross-examined has no seat in that witness box.
concede: The arrears are real, and motive is legitimate context. Context, not proof.
holdout: If the model is the whole case, then there is no case — there is a subscription.
I hold until someone shows me a hand in a till. No one will, because no one ever saw one.
final: Not guilty — and this store owes that woman eight years of apology.

jurors.label: Juror 5
jurors.persona: Wants the prosecution's strongest reading tested fairly before the room buries it.

agree: Tested at full strength, the till-skimming theory still needs the leave weeks to behave — and they do not.
pushback: Be fair to the other side: void-skimming is a real method, and her numbers did fit it.
The auditor was hired by the defence. Weigh that as you weighed the vendor.
concede: Three people to cover her workload. The store's own rostering acquitted her void count.
burden_correct: She does not have to account for a quarter of voids from memory. No honest employee could.
The burden sits with the Crown, and a dashboard cannot lift it.
final: I gave the prosecution its best case. The leave-week numbers ended it on the merits.

jurors.label: Juror 6
jurors.persona: Foreperson; keeps the room from putting the software industry on trial instead of the facts.

agree: Noted for the count — measured and sourced. Good.
That is the standard of argument we vote on.
pushback: We are not here to ban dashboards. We are here to weigh one woman's quarter.
One at a time — and the word 'dystopian' is now rationed.
final: The facts have had their say. Hands, please.

jurors.label: Juror 7
jurors.persona: Drifts with whoever spoke last; the manager's two nights of footage haunt him.

agree: Yes — that version survives repeating. I will keep it.
The three-replacements detail. Every time I stray, someone hands it back to me.
pushback: But the manager watched her for two nights and it looked like method. He was there.
The voids really are three times average, though.
concede: He watched with the score beside the screen. I would have seen method too.
A light scale and a double-counted dock. That is the method — and it is not hers. All right, I'm there.
final: I drifted toward the footage twice. Both times the leave weeks were waiting when I got back.

jurors.label: Juror 8
jurors.persona: Believed the score because it was specific; the leave-week experiment reversed him.

agree: She left for three weeks and the theft got worse. There is no version of guilty that survives that sentence.
A light scale and a double-counted dock — the shrinkage finally has a face, and it is a firmware version.
pushback: Eighty-seven sounded like precision to me. I am telling you why: it had a decimal culture around it and no methods page.
The void statistics deserved an answer. They got one: it was her job.
concede: All right. The score measured her Saturdays. I measured the score. We were both wrong once.
final: The dashboard convicted her in my head for a day. The experiment took one sentence to undo it.

jurors.label: Juror 9
jurors.persona: Quiet doubter; the closed fault tickets bother her more than anything the model said.

agree: Mm. She reported the broken scale twice and they closed the tickets without testing it. Then they prosecuted her for what the scale did.
That is the first witness today who audited instead of assumed.
pushback: Only flagged footage was ever reviewed. The system graded its own homework all quarter.
Everyone is very confident about what till thieves look like on camera.
concede: Her arrears are real. So are half the country's.
final: Quietly: they ignored her fault reports, then blamed her for the fault. Not guilty.

jurors.label: Juror 10
jurors.persona: Wants her to reconcile every void; keeps handing the defence the ledger.

agree: Exactly — three times the void average is not nothing, whatever desk you run.
pushback: Money left that store for a quarter. Somebody carried it out.
burden_drift: Let her reconcile a single week of voids, line by line, and I will listen.
She should have to show us her budget covered that car loan without help.
concede: The judge said it plainly: an observed act. Nobody observed one.
burden_correct: Withdrawn — she owes this room no arithmetic. The Crown owed us a theft, and brought a graph.
final: I demanded her ledger all afternoon. The store's own audit was the only ledger that mattered.

jurors.label: Juror 11
jurors.persona: Undecided; keeps replaying the phrase 'investigative lead' against three months of treating it as proof.

agree: There — the lead became the verdict somewhere around week two, and nobody noticed the promotion.
Right: the score never took leave. She did. The difference is the whole case.
pushback: Could there be two causes — the scale and her? I keep testing the both-things theory.
One more pass at the leave weeks, slowly.
concede: The both-things theory needs her theft to show up somewhere the scale cannot reach. It never does.
final: I tried to keep half the accusation alive. The arithmetic would not share.

jurors.label: Juror 12
jurors.persona: Worked retail through university; knows exactly what a service desk shift contains.

agree: Fair — I ran a service desk. Voids are the job. Two no-sales a shift is trolley coins and locker change, exactly as she said.
And the direction lands it: a score cannot take the oath.
pushback: Watching service-desk footage cold, you would flag every one of us who ever worked one.
The busiest person will always correlate with the busiest losses. That is not insight; it is rostering.
concede: The car loan is the only real thing the prosecution owns. It is not enough to own a person.
final: I did her job for four years. The model would have scored me eighty-seven too.
